title: Case Submission
pageContent:
### OCTcases Excellence in Medical Education Award Awarded annually to two people who submit the best quality cases to our website. Prize is valued at $200 (CAD). | **2021** | Dan Park | Yusuf Ahmed | | -------- | -------- | ----------- | | **2022** | **Jeremy Moreau** | **Korolos Sawires** | ### Have an interesting OCT case? Consider submitting your OCT case to our website as a practice case. Cases will be selected based on quality of write-up and educational value for our readers. Please obtain patient consent for all case submissions. All cases should be de-identified and have the following format: 1. Description of case (patient demographics, HPI, ocular examination) 2. High quality OCT photograph +/- relevant ocular imaging 3. Description of OCT 4. Three or more multiple choice questions 5. Learning objectives Please email all completed cases to: Austin Pereira (tap to reveal email) #### Sample submission ##### Case: Paracentral acute middle maculopathy (PAMM) A 78 year old male presented to clinic with a one month history of a persistent, central scotoma in his left eye after uneventful cataract surgery. Vision was 20/30 OD and 20/25 OS. Pupils were equal and reactive, with no relative afferent pupillary defect. His medications included Clopidogrel, Xarelto, Bisoprolol, and Rosuvastatin. Fundus photograph of the left eye: Fundus photograph was normal in the left eye and an OCT macula was performed, shown below: **Q1: What layer of the retina is abnormal in the OCT?**
  1. Retinal nerve fiber layer
  2. Inner nuclear layer
  3. External limiting membrane
  4. Bruch's membrane
**Answer:** There is a hyper-reflective band (black arrow) at the level of the inner nuclear layer (INL) that spares the outer retina. Remember that the INL is the most anterior hypo-reflective layer of the retina and the deeper hypo-reflective layer corresponds to the outer nuclear layer. **Q2: What is the diagnosis?**
  1. Branch retinal artery occlusion
  2. Paracentral acute middle maculopathy
  3. Stargardt disease
  4. Vitelliform macular dystrophy
  5. Central areolar choroidal dystrophy
**Answer:** Paracentral acute middle maculopathy (PAMM) is a condition characterized by the OCT finding of a hyper-reflective band or bands that preferentially affect the inner nuclear layer of the retina. PAMM has been hypothesized to be secondary to focal ischemia and reported to be associated with numerous vascular diseases. **Q3: What ocular condition can be associated with the development of this OCT finding?**
  1. Macular degeneration
  2. Central serous chorioretinopathy
  3. Central retinal vein occlusion
  4. Corneal ulcer
**Answer:** In a study performed by Rahimy et al. (2014), 5.2% of patients diagnosed with non-ischemic central retinal vein occlusion were found to have evidence of concurrent PAMM. ##### Learning objectives - PAMM is an OCT finding characterized by a hyper-reflective band that affects the inner nuclear layer. - A paracentral scotoma is the most common ocular symptom of PAMM.
